message.content: read #107 and /grillme   . make a new scratchpad with the ultimate design document based on what we discuss.

attachment.content:
- verify-all: Run the full check + clippy sweep across the workspace AND the WASM plugin (panopt-zellij). Use after non-trivial edits, before declaring work done, or when asked to verify the repo builds cleanly. Captures the dual-target dance (workspace builds for host, panopt-zellij builds only for wasm32-wasip1) so you don't have to remember the right manifest-path/target each time.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

aiTitle: Create ultimate design document from discussion

content.text:
I realize that panopt (and it's inspiration: solo(term) are single-user focused.

We want to add an (optional) new layer on top that allows multiple instances of panopt to share their content.
This will allow a user to work on a project in multiple locations, and multiple user to work together with a shared context.

We can think of the heirarchy as:

Execute
Organize
Observe

Where observation gives a view of team-wide activity on all projects.
Organize connects multiple projects for a user and multiple users in a team.
Execute gives users a single 'cockpit' that brings together all the resources they need in order to work efficiently on their projects.


## panopt

Now it does Organize and Execute.
Add the Observe layer on top
Visualize todo dependencies 
Show active projects and agents
Centralize todo and notes across projects / repo clones 

Allows for multi developer coop (unlike solo) 
Wow. That's another,  bad, implication of the solo sense. 
Intended to mean one window. 
But allows for only one dev. ! 

This could be a real product. For dev teams. 

## mapping

the actual panopt application does Execute and some Organize.
the new layer does Observe and some Organize.

content.text: There's no `grillme` skill installed, but the intent is clear — you want me to interrogate scratchpad #107 before writing the design doc. Let me grill you on the foundational choices first.

questions.question: How does data move between panopt instances in the Observe layer?

options.label: Central hub server
options.description: Each panoptd pushes/pulls from a hosted (or self-hosted) observatory service. Strong consistency, simpler auth, requires the hub to be up.

options.label: Git-backed sync
options.description: The .panopt/ filesystem projection becomes the wire format. You commit it, teammates pull it. Free distribution, no new infra, but lossy for live state (locks, presence, agent roster) and racy under concurrent edits.

options.label: P2P / CRDT
options.description: Instances gossip directly (or via a relay) using CRDTs for todos/scratchpads. Offline-first, no central trust, but hard to build and weak for read-only Observe dashboards.

options.label: Hub + local cache
options.description: Hub is the source of truth, each panoptd keeps a local replica and works offline against it, reconciling on reconnect. Most engineering effort, best UX.

questions.question: Two devs clone the same repo to different absolute paths. How does Observe know it's the same project?

options.label: Git remote URL
options.description: Canonicalize origin URL (or first matching remote). Breaks for forks, monorepo subdirs, and repos without a remote.

options.label: Explicit project ID file
options.description: A .panopt/project.toml with a UUID, committed to the repo. Whoever runs `panopt init` first stamps it; everyone else inherits. Explicit, robust, requires a commit.

options.label: Hub-assigned ID + claim
options.description: The Observe hub mints project IDs; each clone claims membership by pointing at the hub. Decouples identity from git but adds a hub dependency even for solo use.

options.label: Both: project.toml + remote fallback
options.description: Prefer the committed UUID; fall back to canonicalized remote when absent. Easy migration path.

questions.question: What's shared by default across the team?

options.label: Opt-in per project
options.description: panopt stays single-user until you explicitly enroll a project in a team/observatory. Conservative — your personal scratchpads never leak — but easy to forget to enroll.

options.label: Opt-in per item
options.description: Project enrollment is required, but each todo/scratchpad has a visibility flag (private/team). Maximum control, lots of UI surface.

options.label: Opt-out per project
options.description: Once a panopt user joins a team, all projects sync by default; you mark specific projects local-only. Friction-light but easy to over-share.

options.label: Two-tier: personal vs team workspace
options.description: Same code dir can have a 'personal' workspace and a 'team' workspace; ws= URL or namespace selects which. Clean mental model, doubles state surface.

questions.question: Is the Observe layer a hosted product, self-hosted, or both?

options.label: Hosted SaaS only (initially)
options.description: Fastest path to a real product. You own auth, billing, the dashboard. Users who can't send code metadata off-box are excluded until you ship self-hosted.

options.label: Self-hosted only (open source)
options.description: Ship the observatory as another crate, users run it. Aligns with panopt's local-first ethos, no business model, slower adoption.

options.label: Self-hosted core + hosted option
options.description: Single binary anyone can run; you also operate a hosted instance. Most flexible, most engineering (multi-tenant + single-tenant code paths).

options.label: Hosted-only with on-prem relay
options.description: Hub is hosted; teams can run an on-prem relay that holds code-identifying data and proxies only metadata to the hub. Compromise for security-sensitive customers.

How does Observe know it's the same project?"="1 sounds easy, but 2 seems more durable. discuss. don't like 3's dependency. what does 4 offer over 2? how can 2 fail (so needing a fallback)?", "What's shared by default across the team?"="Opt-in per project", "Is the Observe layer a hosted product, self-hosted, or both?"="Self-hosted core + hosted option". You can now continue with the user's answers in mind.

aiTitle: Create ultimate design document from discussion

content.text:
Good. Let me push back and unpack those two before moving on.

## Sync: hub + CRDT for bodies

The clean split is **hub-arbitrated for structural state, CRDT for free-text bodies**. They solve different problems:

- **Hub-arbitrated (linearizable):** ID minting (the per-project `projects.next_id` invariant only survives with a single authority), advisory locks (`todo:<N>` needs mutual exclusion — CRDTs can't say "no, someone else has it"), status enums (`pending`/`in_progress`/`done`), membership, roster, presence. Hub is the leader; clients request, hub grants.
- **CRDT (mergeable):** Long text fields — todo body, scratchpad body, comment edits. Two devs typing in different paragraphs of a design doc should merge automatically. Loro or Automerge-rs are the realistic Rust choices; Yjs is JS-first and not a great fit for embedding in `panoptd`.
- **Offline shape:** Local CRDT replica keeps working; lock requests queue and re-arbitrate on reconnect. You can write but you can't *claim* — a non-trivial UX cliff worth being honest about.

The thing CRDTs *don't* buy you: they don't let you work fully offline on a *new* todo (no ID until hub mints one) unless you mint a tentative ID locally and reconcile. Worth deciding.

## Project identity: why 2 needs a fallback (option 4)

You're right that option 1 is brittle alone. Option 2 (committed `.panopt/project.toml` UUID) is the better primary. But it has real failure modes that justify the remote-URL fallback:

1. **Init race.** Two devs on parallel un-pushed branches both run `panopt init` and stamp different UUIDs → merge conflict in a file you didn't expect to conflict.
2. **`.panopt/` gitignored.** Plausible — the rest of `.panopt/*.md` is a read-mostly local projection. If anyone gitignores the dir, identity vanishes with it.
3. **Worktrees / temporary checkouts.** `git worktree add` for a review, or a fresh clone before `panopt init` runs — the file isn't there yet, but the remote is.
4. **File deleted / repo re-init'd.** Someone runs `panopt init` again, mints a new UUID, project "splits."

Remote URL gives graceful degradation: if the UUID file is missing, fall back to canonicalized remote (strip `.git`, normalize `git@host:` vs `https://host/`) to find the existing hub project and *offer* to stamp the UUID. Forks are the genuinely hard case in either model — I'd argue forks should be treated as the *same* project by default (the work is about the same thing) with an explicit "detach" command.

What option 4 buys you over option 2:
- Works on first launch in a fresh clone before init
- Survives accidental file deletion (rediscover by remote)
- Smooth migration: an existing team starts on remote-URL matching, then stamps the UUID into the next commit

The cost is: a second resolver path (~30 lines), and the policy decision of "should the remote fallback ever *create* a new project, or only attach to an existing one?" My answer: never create from a remote — only attach. Creation requires explicit `panopt init` or hub-side enrollment.

---

Now the next layer of questions, based on those answers:

questions.question: How strict are distributed advisory locks under network partitions?

options.label: Hub is sole authority; offline clients can't claim
options.description: Strong correctness: lock state is whatever the hub says. If you're offline you can read and edit text (CRDT), but can't claim `todo:<N>` or start a todo. Simplest model, worst offline UX.

options.label: Optimistic claim, hub adjudicates on reconnect
options.description: Offline client can `todo_start` locally; on reconnect, hub may reject if someone else claimed first (you get a conflict notice). Better UX, requires conflict surfacing in the cockpit.

options.label: Lease-based with TTL
options.description: Hub grants a lock with a short TTL (e.g. 60s) that the client refreshes. Network blip = lock auto-released; another client can grab it. Closer to the existing 30s idle sweep model.

options.label: Soft locks (advisory only, never refused)
options.description: Locks are presence indicators, never block action. Anyone can edit anything; UI shows 'Alice is in this todo'. Matches Google Docs intuition, abandons mutual exclusion.

questions.question: What's the right granularity for visibility *within* an enrolled team project?

options.label: All-or-nothing per project
options.description: If a project is enrolled, every todo/scratchpad in it is visible to all team members. Simple, predictable, no UI for per-item flags. Personal notes need to live in an unenrolled project.

options.label: Per-item private flag
options.description: Each todo/scratchpad has a 'private to me' bit. Simple two-tier model, requires UI affordance and a sync filter.

options.label: Per-item team-and-tag ACL
options.description: Items can be scoped to subgroups (e.g. tag `eng-only`, `leads-only`). Powerful, complex, real ACL plumbing on hub.

options.label: Two-namespace model: personal + team
options.description: Same project dir exposes two MCP workspaces (`ws=...?ns=personal` and `ws=...?ns=team`). User explicitly creates each item in one or the other. Clean mental model, doubles the surface in tools/UI.

questions.question: What is the Observe layer's actual UI?

options.label: Web dashboard (hub-hosted)
options.description: Hub serves a web app: cross-project todo graph, team activity feed, agent presence. Familiar, easy to share links, requires you to build a frontend.

options.label: Cockpit panel (Zellij plugin)
options.description: Another sidebar plugin in the cockpit shows team/observe state. Stays in the terminal, consistent with current panopt, limited screen real estate for graphs.

options.label: Both — web for breadth, cockpit for working state
options.description: Web dashboard for org-wide overview and dependency graphs; cockpit plugin for the slice relevant to the current project. Most work, best ergonomics.

options.label: MCP-first, UI later
options.description: Hub exposes Observe data as MCP tools (`team_active_todos`, `team_agent_roster`, etc.); UI is whatever consumes them (Claude session, custom dashboards). Defers the UI question, makes the data shape the contract.

questions.question: What's the auth/identity model for hub accounts?

options.label: Hub-issued personal tokens + team invites
options.description: Sign up on hub, get a token, paste into local panopt config. Invited to teams by email or invite code. Simplest, no SSO, fine for early adopters.

options.label: OAuth via GitHub/Google
options.description: Identity is your GitHub/Google account; teams map to orgs you belong to. Low friction for devs, ties you to a provider, helpful for git-remote identity matching.

options.label: SSO/SAML for orgs, tokens for solo
options.description: Real orgs use SAML to their IdP; solo users use personal tokens. Required if you want to sell to companies, significant build cost.

options.label: Bring-your-own-identity (self-hosted)
options.description: The self-hosted hub delegates auth to whatever the operator wires up (reverse proxy with their existing SSO, basic auth, etc.). Hub stays auth-agnostic. Fast to ship, harder to centralize team UX.

content.content: User has answered your questions: "How strict are distributed advisory locks under network partitions?"="4", "What's the right granularity for visibility *within* an enrolled team project?"="All-or-nothing per project", "What is the Observe layer's actual UI?"="MCP-first, UI later", "What's the auth/identity model for hub accounts?"="Hub-issued personal tokens + team invites". You can now continue with the user's answers in mind.

content.text: A few more design-blockers before I write the doc.

questions.question: Soft locks change `todo_start` semantics. How should it behave?

options.label: Always succeeds, returns co-workers list
options.description: `todo_start` never returns `{started: false}`. Always flips to in_progress, returns `also_active: [alice, bob]` so the agent can decide whether to proceed. Maximum compatibility, removes a failure mode.

options.label: Succeeds with warning, agent can yield
options.description: Returns `{started: true, also_active: [...]}` and the agent's system prompt convention says: if non-empty, post a comment and stop unless explicitly told to coexist. Soft but principled.

options.label: Keep refusing, soft locks are presence-only for UI
options.description: Locks stay hard for `todo_start` exclusion (existing behavior), but a separate 'presence' channel shows who else is viewing/editing the body. Two concepts, not one.

questions.question: Offline creation of new todos/scratchpads — how do IDs work?

options.label: Refuse create when offline
options.description: Mutations that need a new ID fail until reconnected. Preserves the single per-project id counter invariant cleanly. Worst offline UX.

options.label: Client-side tentative IDs, reconcile on sync
options.description: Local mints a tentative ID (e.g. negative or UUID-prefixed); hub assigns the real numeric ID on reconnect; all references rewrite. Best UX, real engineering work (rewriting references, race on simultaneous mints).

options.label: Per-client ID range pre-allocated by hub
options.description: Hub leases each panopt instance a range (say 1000 IDs) at connect time; instance allocates from its range offline. Reconnect refills. Simple, monotonicity is local, costs you contiguous IDs across users.

questions.question: How does `.panopt/*.md` filesystem projection coexist with team sync?

options.label: Local-only, never committed, gitignored
options.description: Hub is the source of truth; `.panopt/` stays a read-mostly local mirror. Add `.panopt/` to .gitignore on enrollment. Clean separation, you lose the 'read team todos by opening a markdown file in the cloned repo' affordance.

options.label: Committed; hub sync writes to it, git merges resolve conflicts
options.description: Keep current behavior, team sync writes to the same files. Two sources of truth, git conflicts on `.panopt/` are common, but the markdown stays the source-of-truth-y artifact you can grep offline.

options.label: Split: enrolled items in `.panopt-shared/`, personal in `.panopt/`
options.description: Two dirs, two policies. `.panopt-shared/` may be committed (or hub-managed), `.panopt/` is local-only. More structure, clearer story.

questions.question: Where does the Observe layer live in the workspace?

options.label: New crate `panopt-hub` in this workspace
options.description: Single repo, shared types with panopt-tool-surface, easy to keep MCP surface in sync. Couples release cadence; hub deploys when panopt does.

options.label: Separate repo, depends on `panopt-tool-surface` via crates.io
options.description: Independent releases, clean boundary, requires publishing the shared crate. More overhead for an early-stage product.

options.label: New crate here, plus `panopt-hub-client` shared with panoptd
options.description: Hub server is its own crate in-workspace; a thin client crate is what panoptd links against. Three crates, but each has one job. Best long-term shape.

options.label: Succeeds with warning, agent can yield
options.description: Returns `{started: true, also_active: [...]}` and the agent's system prompt convention says: if non-empty, post a comment and stop unless explicitly told to coexist. Soft but principled.

options.label: Keep refusing, soft locks are presence-only for UI
options.description: Locks stay hard for `todo_start` exclusion (existing behavior), but a separate 'presence' channel shows who else is viewing/editing the body. Two concepts, not one.

questions.question: Offline creation of new todos/scratchpads — how do IDs work?

options.label: Refuse create when offline
options.description: Mutations that need a new ID fail until reconnected. Preserves the single per-project id counter invariant cleanly. Worst offline UX.

options.label: Client-side tentative IDs, reconcile on sync
options.description: Local mints a tentative ID (e.g. negative or UUID-prefixed); hub assigns the real numeric ID on reconnect; all references rewrite. Best UX, real engineering work (rewriting references, race on simultaneous mints).

options.label: Per-client ID range pre-allocated by hub
options.description: Hub leases each panopt instance a range (say 1000 IDs) at connect time; instance allocates from its range offline. Reconnect refills. Simple, monotonicity is local, costs you contiguous IDs across users.

questions.question: How does `.panopt/*.md` filesystem projection coexist with team sync?

options.label: Local-only, never committed, gitignored
options.description: Hub is the source of truth; `.panopt/` stays a read-mostly local mirror. Add `.panopt/` to .gitignore on enrollment. Clean separation, you lose the 'read team todos by opening a markdown file in the cloned repo' affordance.

options.label: Committed; hub sync writes to it, git merges resolve conflicts
options.description: Keep current behavior, team sync writes to the same files. Two sources of truth, git conflicts on `.panopt/` are common, but the markdown stays the source-of-truth-y artifact you can grep offline.

options.label: Split: enrolled items in `.panopt-shared/`, personal in `.panopt/`
options.description: Two dirs, two policies. `.panopt-shared/` may be committed (or hub-managed), `.panopt/` is local-only. More structure, clearer story.

questions.question: Where does the Observe layer live in the workspace?

options.label: New crate `panopt-hub` in this workspace
options.description: Single repo, shared types with panopt-tool-surface, easy to keep MCP surface in sync. Couples release cadence; hub deploys when panopt does.

options.label: Separate repo, depends on `panopt-tool-surface` via crates.io
options.description: Independent releases, clean boundary, requires publishing the shared crate. More overhead for an early-stage product.

options.label: New crate here, plus `panopt-hub-client` shared with panoptd
options.description: Hub server is its own crate in-workspace; a thin client crate is what panoptd links against. Three crates, but each has one job. Best long-term shape.

answers.Soft locks change `todo_start` semantics: Keep refusing, soft locks are presence-only for UI
answers.Offline creation of new todos/scratchpad: Client-side tentative IDs, reconcile on sync
answers.How does `.panopt/*.md` filesystem proje: Committed; hub sync writes to it, git merges resolve conflicts
answers.Where does the Observe layer live in the: New crate here, plus `panopt-hub-client` shared with panoptd

lastPrompt: read #107 and /grillme   . make a new scratchpad with the ultimate design document based on what we discuss.

aiTitle: Create ultimate design document from discussion

content.text: I have what I need. Let me load the scratchpad update tool and write the doc.

input.body:
# panopt Observe layer — design

Status: design draft, derived from scratchpad #107 ("panopt evolution") via a /grillme session on 2026-05-29.
Supersedes the speculative notes in #107; #107 remains as the seed.

---

## 1. Problem and framing

panopt today does **Execute** (the cockpit — one user at one workstation, one project at a time) and a small slice of **Organize** (the per-project todo + scratchpad + agent registry surface).

Two real constraints remain:

1. **Single-user.** Inherited from solo(term). The `solo` name itself encodes the limitation — meant to suggest "one window," ended up meaning "one developer." A todo locked by Alice is unreachable to Bob, even when they're collaborating on the same goal.
2. **Single-instance.** Even one developer working from two locations (laptop + desktop, office + home) has two unrelated panopt installs with two unrelated databases. Todos created on one don't exist on the other.

The proposal is to add an optional **Observe** layer above panopt, plus to extend **Organize** to span instances and people, while leaving Execute (the cockpit) unchanged for solo users who don't enroll.

### Layer mapping

| Layer    | Today (panopt)                    | After this design                              |
|----------|-----------------------------------|------------------------------------------------|
| Execute  | The cockpit, fully               | Unchanged                                       |
| Organize | Per-project todos/scratchpads/locks/agent registry | Same surface, now sync-able across instances and team members |
| Observe  | —                                | New: cross-project / cross-user view of todos, dependencies, active agents, presence |

The product framing: dev teams. Cross-project todo dependency visualization, "what's the team doing right now," shared context for AI-agent-assisted work. Single-user installs keep working with nothing on, opt in per project when they want sync.

---

## 2. Architecture at a glance

```
              ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐
              │             panopt-hub (server)             │
              │   axum + websockets                         │
              │   - project registry / membership / ACLs    │
              │   - linearizable state (IDs, locks, status) │
              │   - CRDT replication for body fields        │
              │   - presence / agent roster fan-out         │
              │   - MCP observe_* / team_* tools            │
              └────────────────▲────────────────────────────┘
                               │ wss://hub.example/v1/sync
                               │ (bearer token per panopt instance)
              ┌────────────────┴────────────────┐
              │      panopt-hub-client          │  (new crate, no_std-friendly later)
              │   - WS transport                │
              │   - local CRDT replica          │
              │   - tentative-ID minting        │
              │   - reconnect / replay          │
              └────────────────▲────────────────┘
                               │ (linked in)
              ┌────────────────┴────────────────┐
              │            panoptd              │
              │   panopt-core + MCP surface     │
              │   + hub-client (when enrolled)  │
              └─────────────────────────────────┘
```

Every panoptd is still self-contained. The hub is **optional**; un-enrolled projects keep the existing single-machine semantics with zero code paths touched.

---

## 3. Sync model

Two tracks, picked per field by what they need:

### 3.1 Hub-arbitrated (linearizable)

Anything that needs a single global decision goes through the hub:

- **ID minting.** The per-project `projects.next_id` counter is a hard invariant — a `#N` reference must resolve to exactly one resource across the whole team. Hub is the sole minter for enrolled projects. (See §5 for the offline tentative-ID dance.)
- **Advisory locks.** `todo_start` / `todo_lock` keep their existing semantics: hard exclusion. Hub adjudicates; conflicts return `{started: false, held_by: <name>}` exactly as today. Idle sweep moves from a per-daemon timer to a per-hub-project timer.
- **Status transitions.** `pending` → `in_progress` → `done` is a small enum — last-writer-wins by hub timestamp, with the hub as arbiter. No CRDT.
- **Membership, roster, ACLs.** Hub-owned, immutable from clients except via dedicated MCP tools.
- **Comment thread structural ops.** Adding/deleting comments is a hub-ordered op (comments are addressable by stable id); the *body* of a comment can use the same CRDT path as todo bodies.

### 3.2 CRDT-replicated (mergeable)

Long free-text fields where two people typing in different paragraphs should just merge:

- todo `body`
- scratchpad `body`
- comment `body` edits

**Library choice (open question):** Loro and Automerge-rs are the realistic Rust-native options. Loro has better performance for large documents and Rust ergonomics; Automerge has more battle-testing and a wider ecosystem. Pick after a small benchmark on representative scratchpad sizes. Yjs is rejected — JS-first, embedding cost in panoptd is too high.

The CRDT replica lives in `panopt-hub-client`. Local edits emit ops; ops go to the hub over WebSocket; hub fans out to other connected instances. Hub also persists the CRDT state (so the latest doc survives all-clients-offline).

### 3.3 Presence (ephemeral, fire-and-forget)

A separate channel, distinct from locks. Presence answers "who else is looking at / typing in this todo right now" — not "who owns it." This is the place soft-lock UX lives without polluting the hard-lock semantics of `todo_start`:

- Each panoptd publishes a small heartbeat with `{agent_id, viewing: [todo:42, scratchpad:17]}`.
- Hub fans out to subscribers.
- Cockpit / web UI shows presence chips. No effect on tool calls.

This means presence and locks are **two concepts, not one** — by explicit decision. Locks still refuse. Presence is information-only.

---

## 4. Project identity

Primary: a committed `.panopt/project.toml` with a stamped UUID.

```toml
# .panopt/project.toml — committed
project_id = "01HV...UUIDv7..."
hub        = "https://hub.example.com"   # optional; absent = unenrolled
```

Fallback: canonicalized git remote URL (`https://`/`git@` normalized, `.git` stripped, trailing slash removed). The fallback is **read-only** — it can *attach* a fresh clone to an existing hub project, but it cannot *create* one. Creation requires `panopt init` or hub-side enrollment.

### Why the fallback exists (failure modes of UUID-only)

1. Fresh clone before `panopt init` ran — UUID file isn't there yet.
2. `git worktree add` for a review — same repo, different working dir, identity should follow.
3. UUID file accidentally deleted or repo re-init'd — rediscover by remote rather than orphaning state.
4. Init race on parallel un-pushed branches — fallback gives a recovery surface when the merge conflict in `project.toml` lands.

### Genuinely hard case: forks

Forks share a remote ancestry but not a current remote URL. Default policy: forks are **the same project** (you forked to send a PR; the work is the same work). Explicit `panopt project detach` if a fork is meant to diverge. This is a soft default; revisit after early users hit it.

---

## 5. Offline writes and tentative IDs

Offline is a first-class state — flaky hotel wifi, plane, hub down for maintenance.

### Reads and edits to existing items
- Local CRDT replica keeps working. Body edits queue and replay on reconnect; CRDT merge handles it.
- Status transitions queue with local timestamps; hub adjudicates on reconnect by causal order.

### Creation (the hard case)
- Local mints a **tentative ID** — pick a scheme that's distinguishable from real IDs without polluting the integer space. Proposal: negative integers (`-1`, `-2`, …) at the storage layer with rewrite to positive on commit, and an internal flag `is_tentative` so cockpit/UI can render an indicator.
- All references created offline (blockers, comment threads, tags) use the tentative ID locally.
- On reconnect: client sends a batch of create-ops; hub mints real IDs, returns the mapping; client rewrites every reference atomically.
- If two clients mint tentative `-1` for *different* todos offline, the rewrite handles it — tentative IDs never escape the local scope.

### Lock claims while offline
- Local edits are allowed, but `todo_start` cannot grant a lock — only the hub can.
- Offline `todo_start` returns `{started: false, reason: "offline", retry_when_online: true}`.
- On reconnect, the client may auto-retry; if rejected because someone else claimed, surface the conflict to the agent (comment + status visible).

This is a real UX cliff. Documenting it as part of the design rather than hiding it.

---

## 6. Sharing and ACL

**Opt-in per project.** A panopt install does nothing different until the user runs `panopt project enroll [--hub <url>] [--team <slug>]`. Existing projects stay local. Personal scratchpads in unenrolled projects never leave the machine.

**All-or-nothing within an enrolled project.** Once a project is enrolled, every todo and scratchpad in it is visible to every team member with access. No per-item private flag. Rationale:

- Keeps the MCP surface flat — no visibility field to filter on, no UI flag to forget.
- The natural "I want this private" answer is "use an unenrolled project" — which is the same code path that already exists.
- Mixed-visibility projects are where ACL bugs live; refusing to support them is a feature.

If users push hard on this later, the escape hatch is the two-namespace model (`?ns=personal` vs `?ns=team`) — deferred until demand is concrete.

---

## 7. Authentication and identity

- **Hub-issued personal tokens.** Sign up on the hub, get a token, paste it into `~/.local/share/panopt/hub-token` (or per-project override). Same shape as the existing daemon bearer-token convention.
- **Team invites** by email or invite code. Owner → admin → member roles for the v1 surface.
- No OAuth/SSO for v1. They're on the roadmap once the product has a customer who needs them; SSO/SAML adds weeks of build for an audience that doesn't exist yet.
- Self-hosted operators can bolt their own auth on by terminating at a reverse proxy that injects a header the hub trusts; documented but not packaged.

### Identity flow

```
panoptd starts
  → reads .panopt/project.toml
  → if hub set, reads ~/.local/share/panopt/hub-token
  → opens WS with Authorization: Bearer <token>
  → hub responds with {user_id, display_name, project_role}
  → panoptd uses (user_id × machine_id) as the agent key namespace
```

The existing `$USER-$HOSTNAME` agent key extends to `<user_id>-<hostname>` post-enrollment. Cross-team collisions are impossible by construction.

---

## 8. Deployment model

**Self-hosted core + hosted option.**

- The hub ships as `panopt-hub` — single binary, one-command deploy, SQLite or Postgres backend (start with SQLite, migrate path for Postgres).
- A hosted instance is operated for users who don't want to run infra. Same code, multi-tenant config.
- The on-prem-relay variant (hub hosted, code-identifying data stays on-prem) is deferred — design it in once an enterprise prospect asks.

---

## 9. MCP surface

The Observe layer is **MCP-first**. UI lives downstream of the data shape, not the other way around. New tools, all on the existing `panopt-tool-surface` table:

### Cross-project / team tools (new)

| Tool                  | Purpose                                                       |
|-----------------------|---------------------------------------------------------------|
| `team_projects`       | List projects the current user is a member of                 |
| `team_todo_list`      | List todos across projects (filterable by status, assignee, tag) |
| `team_todo_graph`     | Return the cross-project todo dependency graph                |
| `team_agent_roster`   | All agents currently connected across all team projects       |
| `team_presence`       | Who is viewing/editing what right now                         |
| `team_activity_feed`  | Append-only feed of significant events (todo done, comment added, etc.) |
| `project_enroll`      | Enroll a project with a hub                                   |
| `project_detach`      | Stop syncing a project (local copy persists, hub copy stays)  |
| `project_members`     | Read/manage membership of a project                            |

### Existing per-project tools

Unchanged in name and shape. Internally, when a project is enrolled, mutations also publish to the hub. **The MCP contract is identical for enrolled and unenrolled projects** — agents don't need to know the difference.

### Surface invariant

The existing rule stays: every tool is exactly one `ToolDef` in `panopt_tool_surface::TOOL_SURFACE`, exactly one `Tool` enum variant, exactly one `dispatch_local` arm. The new `team_*` and `project_*` tools follow the same discipline. The `tool_enum_covers_tool_surface` test extends naturally.

---

## 10. Filesystem projection in a multi-user world

Decision: **`.panopt/*.md` stays committed. The hub sync writes to it. Git merges resolve conflicts.**

Rationale:
- The markdown projection is what makes panopt grep-able and human-readable offline. Throwing it away in a team setting loses a core affordance.
- Two write sources (local mutations + hub sync) sound dangerous, but both go through the same `panopt-core` projection code path — the conflict surface is just `.panopt/*.md` files in git, which devs are already equipped to resolve.
- The atomic write invariant (temp file + rename) stays — no partial files for git to see.

Practical follow-ups:
- The hub-sync write happens *before* git sees the change. From git's perspective, only the developer-applied mutations exist.
- A `.panopt/.gitattributes` entry should mark `*.md` as `merge=union` for append-only files (comments, activity log) where union-merge is correct. Todo body files use default merge (and may conflict — that's fine).
- Document the conflict-resolution flow in HANDOFF / README.

---

## 11. Crate layout

Three new pieces:

1. **`panopt-hub`** (new crate, in workspace) — the server. axum + tokio + websockets + SQLite/Postgres. Depends on `panopt-tool-surface` for the shared MCP types and on `panopt-core` for the data model (or a slice of it factored into a shared crate; decide during implementation).
2. **`panopt-hub-client`** (new crate, in workspace) — the client library. WS transport, CRDT replica, tentative ID minting, reconnect, replay. Used by `panoptd` and *anything else* that wants to be a hub participant (future: a CLI viewer, a web UI server, etc.).
3. **Existing `panoptd`** — gains an optional dependency on `panopt-hub-client`. When a project is enrolled, `panoptd` spins up a hub-client task that mirrors `panopt-core` state to the hub and applies incoming ops to it.

The `panopt-core` boundary holds: still no `rmcp`, `axum`, or `tokio` in it. Sync logic lives in `panopt-hub-client`, which is allowed to pull tokio.

The `panopt-zellij` plugin stays as-is (wasm32-wasip1), but gains the ability to render presence/team info by calling the new `team_*` MCP tools through its existing MCP client.

---

## 12. Migration and rollout

- v0: ship `panopt-hub` and `panopt-hub-client` with zero behavior change for existing users. Hub is unreachable, nothing changes.
- v0.1: `panopt project enroll` works against a self-hosted hub. Solo + early-adopter teams kick the tires.
- v0.2: hosted hub instance public-beta. Personal tokens, team invites.
- v1: GA the hosted product. Add SSO conversation only when the first prospect requires it.

Existing solo panopt databases are forward-compatible: a non-enrolled install ignores hub fields entirely.

---

## 13. Open questions / risks

- **CRDT library choice.** Loro vs Automerge — pick after a benchmark on a representative ~10KB scratchpad with concurrent edits. Doesn't block the rest of the design.
- **Fork policy.** "Forks are the same project" is a soft default. Need to revisit when a real user has two parallel forks they actually want separated.
- **Tentative ID reconciliation correctness.** The rewrite-on-reconnect path has obvious corner cases (an offline client referenced a tentative ID in a comment body — body is a CRDT, can the rewrite update it cleanly?). Worth a dedicated proof-of-concept before committing to negative-integer tentative IDs over UUID tentatives.
- **The `db.rs` schema migration story.** Forward-only migrations are fine; the question is whether the hub maintains its own migration chain (likely yes) or shares panopt-core's (unwieldy). Likely answer: hub has its own.
- **Cost of running the hosted hub.** WebSocket fan-out + CRDT persistence at team scale isn't free. Pricing model is out of scope for design but on the radar.
- **The "Observe" name.** Good as a layer concept. Less obvious as a product surface name. "Panopt Observatory"? "Panopt Cloud"? Defer naming until there's a UI to label.

---

## 14. Open question to verify before implementation

Before writing code: re-read `DESIGN.md` and `HANDOFF.md` end-to-end and confirm nothing here violates an invariant they declare. The id-counter and forward-only-migration invariants in particular are load-bearing for this design — if they're documented more strictly than this draft assumes, the hub design needs adjustment, not the invariants.

content.text:
Design doc is in scratchpad **#109** ("panopt Observe layer — design"). It supersedes #107 as the working design, with #107 left as the seed.

Key decisions captured:
- Hub-arbitrated for IDs/locks/status; CRDT for body fields; separate ephemeral presence channel (locks stay hard, presence is its own concept).
- Project identity: committed `.panopt/project.toml` UUID, git-remote fallback that can only attach (never create).
- Opt-in enrollment per project; all-or-nothing visibility inside enrolled projects.
- Hub-issued personal tokens + team invites; self-hosted core with a hosted option.
- MCP-first surface (`team_*`, `project_*`); UI deferred.
- `.panopt/*.md` stays committed; hub writes through the same projection path; git resolves conflicts.
- Three crates: new `panopt-hub` (server), new `panopt-hub-client` (client lib), `panoptd` links the client.

Open items I flagged rather than guessed: CRDT lib choice (Loro vs Automerge bench-off), fork policy as a soft default, tentative-ID rewrite corner cases, hub schema migration ownership, product naming.
